title: Putting It All Together: Stride, Padding, Dilation
concept: Dilation doesn't need a new formula — it changes the effective_filter_size term inside the same output-size formula, since a dilated filter reaches further than its raw weight count suggests.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In the output size formula, what does the 'floor' step do?

options.text: Rounds the division result down to the nearest whole number, since a filter can't sit on a fractional position
Rounds the division result up to the nearest whole number
Removes the stride from the equation entirely

exercises.explain: Right — floor rounds down. Now let's fold dilation, from a few lessons back, into this very formula.

cards.title: One dial you haven't plugged in yet
cards.body: You've got the formula solid for stride and padding. But a few lessons back you met dilation — filters with gaps punched into them. Does the formula still work? Yes, once you know what number to plug in for the filter size.

cards.title: Effective filter size
cards.body: A dilated filter doesn't use its raw size in the formula — it uses its effective filter size: effective_size = filter_size + (filter_size − 1) × (dilation − 1). In plain words: take the filter size, and for every gap the dilation adds between weights, stretch the footprint out that much further. Then plug effective_size into the output formula wherever filter_size used to go.

cards.title: Spread fingers cover more table
cards.body: Hold your hand flat on a table — five fingers, one tight cluster. Now spread your fingers apart as far as they'll go. Still five fingers (five weights), but your hand now spans way more table space. A dilated filter is the spread-fingers version: same weight count, bigger reach — and it's that reach, not the weight count, that decides how much room it needs.

cards.title: A 3x3 filter with dilation 2
cards.body: A 3x3 filter (filter_size = 3) with dilation = 2 has effective size 3 + (3−1)×(2−1) = 3 + 2 = 5. It behaves like a 5x5 filter for sizing purposes, even though it only has 9 real weights. Plug that into the formula: on a 9x9 input, padding 0, stride 1: (9 + 0 − 5) / 1 = 4, floor(4) + 1 = 5. Output: 5x5 — exactly what a plain 5x5 filter would produce.

cards.title: Same weight count doesn't mean same footprint
cards.body: It's easy to assume a 3x3 dilated filter must produce the same output size as a regular 3x3 filter, since it's 'still just 3x3.' It doesn't — the gaps between weights count toward the footprint. A dilated 3x3 filter with dilation 2 shrinks the output exactly like a 5x5 filter would, not a 3x3 one.

cards.title: Regular 3x3 vs. Dilated 3x3 (d=2)
cards.body: Same weight count, very different footprint:

exercises.prompt: A 5x5 filter has dilation 3. What's its effective filter size?
True or false: A dilated filter uses more learnable weights than the same filter with dilation 1.
exercises.explain: False — dilation only spreads the SAME weights further apart; it adds zero new weights, only a bigger footprint.

exercises.sentence: A dilated filter's effective size is bigger than its raw filter size because dilation adds ___ between the weights.
exercises.explain: Gaps — those gaps between weights are exactly what stretches a dilated filter's real footprint beyond its raw size.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly applies dilation inside the output-size formula?

options.text: Compute the effective filter size first (filter_size + (filter_size−1)×(dilation−1)), then use THAT in place of filter_size in the normal output formula
Dilation multiplies the final output size directly, after the whole formula is already computed
Dilation is ignored by the output size formula entirely, since it doesn't add real weights

exercises.explain: Right — effective filter size replaces the raw filter size inside the same formula; nothing else about the formula changes.

exercises.prompt: A CNN layer uses a 3x3 filter with dilation 2 on a 15x15 input, padding 0, stride 1. What's the output size?

options.text: 11x11 — first find the effective filter size (5x5), then run the normal formula: (15 − 5)/1 = 10, floor(10) + 1 = 11
13x13 — since dilation only adds 2 to the final output, not the filter size
15x15 — dilation doesn't affect output size at all
7x7, using the raw 3x3 filter size directly in the formula and ignoring dilation

exercises.explain: Right — effective size is 5x5, then (15 + 0 − 5)/1 = 10, floor(10) + 1 = 11. Output: 11x11. Now that stride, padding, AND dilation all plug cleanly into one formula, let's get faster at doing this math by hand.
